commands.description: Identify file type, architecture, linking, and whether stripped
Display ELF header (entry point, architecture, type)
List all sections (.text, .data, .rodata, .bss, etc.)
Display symbol table (function names, global variables)
Show program headers (segments loaded at runtime)
Disassemble all executable sections
Disassemble using Intel syntax (easier to read)
Display symbol table entries
Check security features (NX, PIE, RELRO, canary, RPATH)
List shared library dependencies
Extract printable ASCII strings (min 4 chars by default)
Extract strings with minimum length of 8 characters
Extract UTF-16LE strings (common in Windows PE binaries)
Trace library calls (strcmp, strlen, printf, malloc, etc.)
Trace library calls with string display up to 200 chars
Trace system calls (open, read, write, mmap, etc.)
Trace system calls following child processes
Trace only file-related system calls
List symbols from object file (functions and variables)
List dynamic symbols (imported/exported functions)
Import a binary for analysis (auto-detects format and architecture)
Run all automatic analysis passes (functions, xrefs, types)
View all identified strings and their cross-references
Browse all identified symbols (functions, labels, variables)
View control flow graph of the current function
Search for specific strings across the entire binary
Search for specific constant values (hex, decimal)
Find all locations that reference the selected address
Rename a function or variable for clarity
Change the data type of a variable in the decompiler
Jump to a specific address or symbol
Modify instruction bytes directly in the listing
Start GDB with the target binary
List all known functions
Disassemble the main function
Set breakpoint at a specific address
Set breakpoint at the start of main
Run the program (or restart if already running)
Run with input redirected from a file
Step one instruction (step over calls)
Step one instruction (step into calls)
Continue execution until next breakpoint
Display all register values
Examine 20 words (hex) on the stack
Examine memory at address as a string
Disassemble 10 instructions from current instruction pointer
Change instruction pointer (skip code)
Write a value to a memory address
(GEF) Show memory map of the process
(GEF) Dereference and display 20 stack entries
Open a binary in radare2
Open binary in debug mode
Analyze all (functions, references, strings)
List all functions found by analysis
Seek to the main function
Print disassembly of the current function
Print disassembly of main from anywhere
Enter visual graph mode (control flow graph)
List strings in the data sections
List all strings in the entire binary
Show cross-references to an address
Print 64 bytes as hex dump at address
Set breakpoint at address (debug mode)
Continue execution (debug mode)
Show register values (debug mode)
Write a NOP instruction at address (patching)
Decompile .pyc file back to Python source code
Decompile .pyc using pycdc (supports newer Python versions)
Show Python bytecode disassembly of a script

commands.command: python3 -c "import dis, marshal; f=open('file.pyc','rb'); f.read(16); dis.dis(marshal.load(f))"
commands.description: Disassemble bytecode from a .pyc file directly
Extract files from a PyInstaller-packed executable
Read the magic number to identify the Python version of a .pyc file
Check if an executable is PyInstaller-packed
Check for ptrace-based anti-debugging (PTRACE_TRACEME)
Detect if binary calls ptrace to prevent debugging
Bypass ptrace anti-debug by preloading a library that stubs ptrace
(GDB) Patch ptrace to return immediately (0xc3 = ret)
(GDB) Break on ptrace syscall to inspect and modify its behavior
Unpack a UPX-packed binary
Check if a binary is UPX-packed
Check if current process is being traced (anti-debug check)
